metadata.file_name: 1. Lee & Lee. (2025). An Extended Dataset of Educational Quality Across Countries (1970–2023) 3.pdf
metadata.title: An Extended Dataset of Educational Quality Across Countries (1970–2023)

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: This paper is a data construction/harmonization study rather than an individual-level ILSA micro-data analysis. It builds a cross-country panel (101 countries, 1970–2023) by harmonizing mathematics and science test scores to the TIMSS 1995 metric and imputing missing country-year observations. For the working-age educational quality index (ages 15–64) for 2015 and 2023, the authors explicitly incorporate population weights (age-cohort population shares) and use estimated wage-return parameters to aggregate cohort-level normalized test scores into Q. The manuscript does not report the use of student-level sampling/survey weights (e.g., W_FSTUWT) or replicate-weight methods (BRR/jackknife) because the unit of analysis is country-year aggregates rather than a representative student sample.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: The study does not analyze PISA/TIMSS student-level achievement outcomes with Plausible Values (PVs). Instead, it constructs harmonized country-year test-score series (Tscore_INT and Tscore_ML) and imputes missing country-year observations using linear interpolation/extrapolation and a country-level LASSO prediction model based on World Bank indicators. Because the missingness addressed is at the country-year panel level (not item-level missing PVs in an ILSA micro dataset), PV-specific handling is not applicable, and the paper does not describe a PV-combining or PV-imputation protocol in the PV sense.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: The dataset construction includes 101 countries with observations spanning 1970–2023, but the paper does not provide a student-level analytic sample. It excludes countries from the final sample based on (i) absence of nationally representative samples (explicitly citing China and India as examples) and (ii) missing key national indicators in the World Bank education and economic datasets required for panel construction and imputation. The harmonized panel is anchored to TIMSS 1995 (mean=500, SD=100) and uses 12 key assessment years (1970, 1980, 1984, 1990, 1995, 1999, 2003, 2007, 2011, 2015, 2019, 2023). Missing country-year observations (581 out of 1212 potential observations; 48%) are imputed using either linear interpolation/extrapolation (Tscore_INT) or LASSO regression predictions (Tscore_ML).

main_findings.performance_metrics: LASSO model: RMSE = 17.5, R² = 0.912 (with 80% training / 20% validation and grid search with 10-fold cross-validation); out-of-sample RMSE = 15.7, R² = 0.905
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using the harmonized cross-country test-score panel (1970–2023) anchored to the TIMSS 1995 metric, the study leveraged economic and education indicators to predict missing country-year test scores via LASSO regression, finding strong predictive performance (R² ≈ 0.91; RMSE ≈ 17.5 in-sample and ≈ 15.7 out-of-sample). This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: This paper constructs an extended educational-quality dataset for 101 countries from 1970 to 2023 by harmonizing mathematics and science test scores to the TIMSS 1995 scale and imputing missing country-year values. It uses two complementary imputation approaches: linear interpolation/extrapolation (Tscore_INT) and machine-learning prediction with LASSO regression (Tscore_ML) based on 501 fully observed World Bank economic and education indicators. The LASSO model is trained with an 80/20 split and grid search with 10-fold cross-validation, achieving RMSE = 17.5 and R² = 0.912, and out-of-sample RMSE = 15.7 with R² = 0.905. The two imputation methods are highly consistent (correlation = 0.967), supporting the reliability of the balanced panel. Because the work is a dataset construction/harmonization study (country-year aggregates) rather than an individual-level ILSA PV-based analysis, PV handling and student survey-weighting are not applicable in the usual ILSA sense.
